skill.name: condition-based-waiting
skill.description: Replace arbitrary test sleeps with condition polling that waits for real state, event, count, or file changes.
skill.summary: Improve flaky asynchronous tests by waiting for observable conditions instead of guessed delays.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution and weak-crypto findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, timeout strings, and ordinary parameter names. No prompt injection, network access, secret access, malware behavior, or actual shell execution was found. A low-risk filesystem polling example appears in documentation and should be used only with bounded timeouts.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external command locations are Markdown code fences, inline examples, or TypeScript timeout messages. I found no shell execution, process spawning, or command interpolation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations contain Markdown fences, documentation text, or Error template strings. No executable shell API or Ruby backtick execution is present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance labels do not match the file context. The cited lines describe test waiting, descriptions, IDs, or timeout handling, not cryptographic algorithms or system probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code contains no hashing, encryption, host enumeration, or OS reconnaissance. The scanner appears to have matched benign substrings and documentation language.

low_findings.title: Documentation Mentions Filesystem Polling
low_findings.description: The skill includes a wait-for-file example using a filesystem existence check. This is a legitimate testing pattern, but consumers should avoid unbounded polling and sensitive paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access appears only as a documentation example, not as executable package behavior. It is still a real capability suggestion that can touch local paths if copied into tests.

content.value_statement: Flaky tests often fail because they guess how long async work needs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to replace sleeps with bounded condition polling.
actual_capabilities: Identifies arbitrary sleeps and timeout waits in test flows.
Recommends polling for events, state changes, counts, and file existence.
Provides reusable waitFor helper patterns with timeout errors.
Shows domain-specific event waiting helpers for asynchronous test systems.
Explains when a documented fixed delay is still appropriate.
limitations: It does not run tests or prove a race condition by itself.
It does not replace timing assertions for debounce or throttle behavior.
It requires a reliable condition source, such as events or state.
Filesystem polling examples need careful path and timeout choices.

use_cases.title: Fix CI-Only Test Failures
use_cases.description: Replace guessed sleeps with conditions that wait until the tested system reaches the required state.

use_cases.title: Review Async Test Utilities
use_cases.description: Convert repeated timeout patterns into shared wait helpers with clear failure messages.

use_cases.title: Improve Event-Driven Tests
use_cases.description: Wait for specific events, event counts, or event predicates before making assertions.

prompt_templates.title: Find Flaky Sleeps
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small test file
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this test file for arbitrary sleeps. Point out each sleep that should become condition-based waiting.

prompt_templates.title: Replace One Timeout
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused refactor of one flaky assertion
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this test section to wait for the real condition before asserting. Keep the behavior unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Create Wait Helpers
prompt_templates.scenario: Shared utility design for a test suite
prompt_templates.prompt: Design reusable wait helpers for these async test patterns. Include timeout handling and clear failure messages.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Timing Exceptions
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of deliberate timing tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Separate necessary timing assertions from avoidable sleeps. Explain which delays must remain and why.

output_examples.input: A test waits 300 milliseconds before checking that two events exist.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend waiting until two matching events are observed, then asserting on the collected events.

output_examples.input: A test waits for a background process to create an output file.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest bounded polling for file existence with a clear timeout message.

output_examples.input: A debounce test waits for a known interval before checking output.
output_examples.output: The skill would keep the timed wait only if the delay verifies the debounce contract and is documented.

best_practices: Wait for the exact observable condition needed by the assertion.
Set bounded timeouts with error messages that name the missing condition.
Poll at a reasonable interval to reduce CPU load during test runs.
anti_patterns: Do not use sleeps as guesses for async completion.
Do not poll forever when a condition might never happen.
Do not cache state before the polling loop and assert on stale data.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps replace fragile sleeps in tests with waits for real state, event, count, or file conditions.

faq.question: Can this skill remove every timeout?
faq.answer: No. Timeouts are still needed as failure bounds and for tests that verify timing behavior.

faq.question: Does it execute commands?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files provide documentation and TypeScript examples, not executable shell commands.

faq.question: Is filesystem access required?
faq.answer: No. Filesystem polling is only one optional pattern for tests that wait for generated files.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: When should a fixed delay remain?
faq.answer: Keep a fixed delay only when the test verifies timing behavior and the reason is documented.
